name: Fail-Fast

x.failureMitigated: Silent propagation of corrupt or invalid state that surfaces later as a distant, hard-to-diagnose failure.
x.detectionPoint: The earliest boundary where an invariant or precondition can be checked — on input, at construction, or at function entry.
x.recovery: None locally, by design: abort/throw/panic immediately and let a supervisory layer (process restart, request retry, circuit breaker) recover at the edge.
x.operationalCost: The checks are cheap; the real cost is availability if applied to recoverable conditions or used without a restart/supervision strategy.
falseConfidence: Crashing on user-recoverable input degrades UX and availability
Fail-fast without supervision yields crash loops
Assertions compiled out in release silently disable the guarantee

def: Detecting and reporting errors immediately at their source rather than continuing in a bad state.
problem: Prevent corrupt state from propagating and turning a clear bug into a distant, mysterious one.
mechanism: Validate preconditions early; on violation, abort/throw/panic immediately instead of limping on with invalid data.
limits: Crashing can hurt availability if overused
Must be paired with supervision/recovery at boundaries
failures: Failing fast on recoverable input → poor UX/availability
whenNot: At system edges where degradation is preferable
myths: "Fail-fast means crash everywhere" — fail fast internally, degrade gracefully at the edge
